title: Hear the First Book of HomerБ─≥s Iliad Read Aloud in the Original Greek
description:
in History, Language, Literature |   	June 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You can, of course, learn the Greek lanбґguage as itБ─≥s spoбґken today.
body:
in History, Language, Literature |   	June 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You can, of course, learn the Greek lanбґguage as itБ─≥s spoбґken today. You can also learn Greek as it was spoбґken in antiqбґuiбґty Б─■ and as it was, until fairбґly recentбґly in hisбґtorбґiбґcal time, taught to stuбґdents in the modбґern West. But itБ─≥s a fairбґly difбґferбґent endeavбґor again to learn Greek as Homer spoke it. The fact of the matбґter is that no human being ever realбґly spoke like Achilles, Agamemбґnon, Odysseus, Peneбґlope, or any of the othбґer charбґacбґters in the Iliбґad andб═Odyssey. HomeбґrБ─≥s many litбґerбґary achieveбґments through these works include the creбґation and comбґmand of a kind of synбґtheбґsized poetбґic Greek, comбґbinбґing qualбґiбґties of regionбґal Ionбґic and Aeolic dialects with varбґiбґous forms and expresбґsions that were outбґdatбґed even in the eighth cenбґtuбґry BC. If it served the meter, Homer used it.
Needбґless to say, when most of us attempt to read Homer aloud in the origбґiбґnal, we get it all or mostбґly wrong, even if weБ─≥re familбґiar with modбґern Greek. WeБ─≥d have to spend a long time indeed in the world of clasбґsiбґcists before hearбґing a more accuбґrate recordбґing than the one above, delivбґered by a YouTuбґber called Thomas Whichelбґlo.
On his chanбґnel, Whichelбґlo speбґcialбґizes in perбґformбґing venбґerбґaбґble litбґerбґary texts with a proбґnunбґciбґaбґtion and cadence as close to periбґod-accuбґrate as posбґsiбґble, often in the origбґiбґnal lanбґguage, someбґtimes with his own musiбґcal accomбґpaбґniбґment. HeБ─≥s done readбґings of theб═Bible, Shakeбґspeare, Keats, and Wilde, but none so far has been so popбґuбґlar as his renбґdiбґtion of the first book of the Iliбґad, accomбґpaбґnied by subбґtiбґtles of HomeбґrБ─≥s text and an Engбґlish transбґlaбґtion.
A Greek here in 2026 with no parбґticбґuбґlar knowlбґedge of the clasбґsiбґcal lanбґguage may underбґstand a quarбґter of the indiбґvidбґual words Whichelбґlo uses, and maybe half of them in cerбґtain pasбґsages. Actuбґalбґly being able to folбґlow the stoбґry, howбґevбґer, is anothбґer matбґter. Still, you can get a surбґprisбґing amount out of the video even if you underбґstand nothбґing at all, since Whichelбґlo is aimбґing not just for linбґguisбґtic accuбґraбґcy, but also emoбґtionбґal resбґoбґnance in his delivбґery. Ignore his glassбґes, butбґton-down shirt, microбґphone, and winбґdow frame, and you could almost be sitбґting around a campбґfire with him nearбґly 30 cenбґturies ago. Note, also, that the comбґmenters include genбґuine clasбґsiбґcists who call his the best readбґing theyБ─≥ve ever heard Б─■ as well as viewбґers, creбґdenбґtialed or othбґerбґwise, eager to hear him name all those mighty Achaean ships in Book 2.
Relatбґed conбґtent:
Watch All 18,225 Lines of the Iliбґad Read by 66 Actors in a Marathon Event For an Audiбґence of 50,000
Hear What HomerБ─≥s Odyssey Soundбґed Like When Sung in the Origбґiбґnal Ancient Greek
Learn Ancient Greek in 118 Free Lessons: A Free Online Course from Branбґdeis & Harбґvard
The Ancient Greeks: A Free Online Course from Wesбґleyan Uniбґverбґsiбґty
Lisбґten to The Epic of Gilбґgamesh Being Read in its Origбґiбґnal Ancient Lanбґguage, Akkaбґdiбґan
Hear Beowulf and Gawain and the Green Knight Read in Their Origбґiбґnal Old and Midбґdle Engбґlish by an MIT Medievalбґist
Based in Seoul,б═Colбґinб═Marshallб═writes and broadбґcasts on cities, lanбґguage, and culбґture. HeБ─≥s the author of the newsletбґterб═Books on Citiesб═as well as the booksб═М∙°Й╣ґ Л ■Л∙Ґ Й╦┬Лї─б═(No Sumбґmaбґrizбґing Korea) andб═Koreбґan Newtro.б═Folбґlow him on the social netбґwork forбґmerбґly known as Twitбґter atб═@colinmarshall.

title: How Conflict Helped Create Pink FloydБ─≥s Б─°Comfortably NumbБ─² and Its Legendary Guitar Solos
description:
in Music |   	June 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even among the most acclaimed albums ever recordбґed, not a sinбґgle one is perбґfect. That goe
body:
in Music |   	June 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even among the most acclaimed albums ever recordбґed, not a sinбґgle one is perбґfect. That goes more so for the releasбґes of what I call the Б─°heroбґic age of the album,Б─² which enjoyed its zenith around the late sevбґenбґties. Not coinбґciбґdenбґtalбґly, 1979 was the year that Pink Floyd put out The Wall, a rock opera whose sprawl across two discs deals with themes rangбґing from the bombбґings of the Secбґond World War to drug depenбґdenбґcy to fasбґcist impulsбґes to the isoбґlaбґtion of superбґstarбґdom. This ambiбґtion was repaid: The Wallб═soon became the best-sellбґing douбґble album of all time, despite havбґing been received with at least a meaбґsure of ambivaбґlence over the grandбґness, or perбґhaps grandiosбґiбґty, of the scale of its proбґducбґtion and the tone of its narбґraбґtive.
Yet those few preбґpared to call The Wall an artisбґtic failбґure must nevбґerбґtheбґless acknowlбґedge how much impresбґsive work it realбґly does conбґtain. Of its popбґuбґlarбґly appreбґciбґatбґed achieveбґments, perбґhaps the most memбґoбґrable is David GilmourБ─≥s guiбґtar solo, or rather the guiбґtar solos, on Б─°Comбґfortбґably Numb,Б─² a song about being medбґicalбґly revived from a subбґstance-induced stuбґpor moments before givбґing a conбґcert.
They cerбґtainбґly stuck in my own head in sevбґenth grade, when my music teacher assigned our class term paper anaбґlyzбґing the album, and kept popбґping back into it over the subбґseбґquent decades. Б─°His playбґing is so lyriбґcal,Б─² says YouTuбґber David Hartбґley in his new video about the makбґing of Б─°Comбґfortбґably Numb.Б─² Б─°The way he plays each note is in a way that you can almost sing it, and the way he uses phrasбґes is so simбґple, and so beauбґtiбґful.Б─²
These solos were recordбґed in a conбґtext of less-than-smooth sailбґing for the Floyd: as weБ─≥ve preбґviбґousбґly feaбґtured here on Open Culбґture, Б─°Comбґfortбґably NumbБ─² was the prodбґuct of anothбґer arguбґment puncбґtuбґatбґing the long-frayбґing partбґnerбґship between Gilmour and lead singer Roger Waters, for whom The Wall was a way of renбґderбґing his own life expeбґriбґences and perбґcepбґtions in musiбґcal form. But as someбґtimes hapбґpens, conбґflict Б─■ in this case, between two comбґpetбґing and starkбґly difбґferбґent conбґcepts of the song, whose evoбґluбґtion Hartбґley explains with demo recordбґings and interбґview clips Б─■ proбґduced a greater result than any one artistБ─≥s vision. It all arrives at what Hartбґley calls Б─°posбґsiбґbly the greatбґest guiбґtar solo of all time,Б─² which closбґes out side three, and indeed the most fruitбґful era of Gilmour and WatersБ─≥ colбґlabбґoбґraбґtion. Even those who canБ─≥t take The Wall too seriбґousбґly have to admit that life isnБ─≥t necбґesбґsarбґiбґly easy for a rock star, much less for two of them in the same stuбґdio.
Relatбґed conбґtent:
How Pink FloydБ─≥s Б─°Comбґfortбґably NumbБ─² Was Born From an Arguбґment Between Roger Waters & David Gilmour
The Hisбґtoбґry of the Elecбґtric Guiбґtar Solo: A Sevбґen-Part Series
Pink Floyd Songs Played Splenбґdidбґly on a Harp Guiбґtar: Б─°Comбґfortбґably Numb,Б─² Б─°Wish You Were HereБ─² & More
Oxford Sciбґenбґtist Explains the Physics of Playбґing Elecбґtric Guiбґtar Solos
David Gilmour & David Bowie Sing Б─°Comбґfortбґably NumbБ─² Live (2006)
The Evoбґluбґtion of the Rock Guiбґtar Solo: 28 Solos, Spanбґning 50 Years, Played in 6 Fun Minбґutes
Based in Seoul,б═Colбґinб═Marshallб═writes and broadбґcasts on cities, lanбґguage, and culбґture. HeБ─≥s the author of the newsletбґterб═Books on Citiesб═as well as the booksб═М∙°Й╣ґ Л ■Л∙Ґ Й╦┬Лї─б═(No Sumбґmaбґrizбґing Korea) andб═Koreбґan Newtro.б═Folбґlow him on the social netбґwork forбґmerбґly known as Twitбґter atб═@colinmarshall.

title: Kurt Vonnegut Diagrams the Shape of All Stories in a MasterБ─≥s Thesis Rejected by U. Chicago
description:
in Design, Literature, Writing |   	June 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Б─°What has been my pretбґtiбґest conбґtriбґbuбґtion to the culбґture?Б─² asked Kurt
body:
in Design, Literature, Writing |   	June 3rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Б─°What has been my pretбґtiбґest conбґtriбґbuбґtion to the culбґture?Б─² asked Kurt Vonбґnegut in his autoбґbiбґogбґraбґphy Palm Sunбґday. His answer? His masterБ─≥s theбґsis in anthroбґpolбґoбґgy for the Uniбґverбґsiбґty of Chicaбґgo, Б─°which was rejectбґed because it was so simбґple and looked like too much fun.Б─² The eleбґgant simбґplicбґiбґty and playбґfulбґness of VonnegutБ─≥s idea is exactбґly its endurбґing appeal. The idea is so simбґple, in fact, that Vonбґnegut sums the whole thing up in one eleбґgant senбґtence: Б─°The funбґdaбґmenбґtal idea is that stoбґries have shapes which can be drawn on graph paper, and that the shape of a givбґen societyБ─≥s stoбґries is at least as interбґestбґing as the shape of its pots or spearбґheads.Б─²б═In 2011, we feaбґtured the video below of Vonбґnegut explainбґing his theбґoбґry, Б─°The Shapes of Stoбґries.Б─² We can add to the dry wit of his lesбґson the picбґto-infoбґgraphбґic by graphбґic designбґer Maya Eilбґam above, which strikбґingбґly illusбґtrates, with examбґples, the varбґiбґous stoбґry shapes Vonбґnegut described in his theбґsis. (Read a conбґdensed verбґsion here.)
The preбґsenбґter who introбґduces VonбґnegutБ─≥s short lecбґture tells us that Б─°his sinбґguбґlar view of the world applies not just to his stoбґries and charбґacбґters but to some of his theбґoбґries as well.Б─² This I would affirm. When it comes to puzбґzling out the import of a stoбґry IБ─≥ve just read, the last perбґson I usuбґalбґly turn to is the author. But when it comes to what ficбґtion is and does in genбґerбґal, I want to hear it from writбґers of ficбґtion. Some of the most endurбґing litбґerбґary figбґures are expert writбґers on writбґing. Vonбґnegut, a masбґter comбґmuбґniбґcaбґtor, ranks very highбґly among them. Does it do him a disбґserбґvice to conбґdense his ideas into what look like high-res, low-readбґabilбґiбґty workбґplace safeбґty graphбґics? On the conбґtrary, I think.


	

Though the design may be a litбґtle slick for VonбґnegutБ─≥s unapoloбґgetбґiбґcalбґly indusбґtriбґal approach, heБ─≥d have appreбґciбґatбґed the slightбґly corny, slightбґly macabre boilбґerбґplate iconogбґraбґphy. His work turns a susбґpiбґcious eye on overбґcomбґpliбґcatбґed posбґturбґing and chamбґpiбґons unsenбґtiбґmenбґtal, Midбґwestбґern directбґness. VonnegutБ─≥s short, trade pubбґliбґcaбґtion essay, Б─°How to Write With Style,Б─² is as sucбґcinct and pracбґtiбґcal a stateбґment on the subбґject in exisбґtence. One will encounter no more ruthбґlessбґly effiбґcient list than his Б─°Eight Rules for Writбґing Ficбґtion.Б─² But itБ─≥s in his Б─°Shapes of StoбґriesБ─² theбґoбґry that I find the most insight into what ficбґtion does, in brilбґliantбґly simбґple and funбґny ways that anyбґone can appreбґciбґate.
Note: An earбґliбґer verбґsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
Relatбґed Conбґtent:б═
Kurt Vonбґnegut Explains Б─°How to Write With StyleБ─²
The Shape of A Stoбґry: Writбґing Tips from Kurt Vonбґnegut
Kurt Vonбґnegut Offers 8 Tips on How to Write Good Short Stoбґries
Kurt Vonбґnegut: Where Do I Get My Ideas From? My Disбґgust with Civбґiбґlizaбґtion
Josh Jones is a writer and musiбґcian based in Durham, NC.б═

title: Don't Feed the Ducks! A Zany Animation Predicts the Absurd Outcomes of Ignoring the Rules
description: How many people actually heed the warnings about not feeding ducks waddling around public parks? If youБ─≥ve taken a flippant approach to these guid
body:
How many people actually heed the warnings about not feeding ducks waddling around public parks? If youБ─≥ve taken a flippant approach to these guidelines in the past, we recommend you watch AJ JeffriesБ─≥ new animation, Б─°DUCKS.Б─² What opens as an innocuous jaunt around a pond quickly turns into a dark comedy full of strange contortions and feathered villains sure to pop into your head the next time you throw a chunk of bread.
Jeffries is also behind this ridiculous story of a struggling horse, and you can find more of his work onб═Instagram,б═Vimeo, orб═Behance.

title: Ruth Borgenicht Links Thousands of Ceramic Rings in Elaborate Chainmail Sculptures
description: When New Jersey-based artist and educator Ruth Borgenicht attended university, she studied mathematics. But as she shares in a statement: Б─°Love of
body:
When New Jersey-based artist and educator Ruth Borgenicht attended university, she studied mathematics. But as she shares in a statement: Б─°Love of math was not enough to invent new ideas in this fieldБ─■unfortunately for me, that also required genius.Б─² As the saying goes, when a door closes, a window opens, and for Borgenicht, that opportunity came in the form of ceramics.
Through precise forms and meticulous stoneware arrangements, the artist creates wall-hung and tabletop sculptures that are in some cases even kinetic, alternating between basket-like vessels and sturdy, elegantly nestled abstract forms. They often hang tapestry-like from a series of nails or unfurl into three-dimensional biomorphic forms, such as her Centipedes series, which mimic the long, multi-legged bodies of the oft-abhorred arthropods.
Б─°Cubed Rings (large),Б─² wood soda fired stoneware, 13 x 12 x 8 inches
The exactitude of geometry and other mathematical concepts are foundational to BorgenichtБ─≥s work, but she revels in their relationships to nature and the handmade. Б─°Math, at least at the undergraduate level, often involves learning known modes of thinking and working along well-worn paths,Б─² she tells Colossal. Б─°Clay, by contrast, records the imprint of every touch, pressure, and gesture that comes into contact with it. No two objects shaped by human hands can ever be identical.Б─²
Inspiration for creating chainmail sculptures struck after Borgenicht visited the Philadelphia Museum of ArtБ─≥s medieval exhibition space nearly four decades ago. Б─°It occurred to me that it would be an absurd thing to try and make chainmailБ─■or really any kind of moveable meshБ─■from clay,Б─² she says. Б─°So of course I had to give it a try.Б─²
Borgenicht is currently working toward an exhibition at NL=US Art in Rotterdam which is slated to open in February 2027 and will include a foray into 3D printing. Б─°IБ─≥ve been thinking about how forces, repercussions, and influence reverberate through vast interconnected systems,Б─² she says. Б─°A small decision in one arena can ripple outward and profoundly affect a completely different sphere on the other side of the globe.Б─²
See more of BorgenichtБ─≥s work on Instagram.
Б─°Triple Mirage,Б─² stoneware, 27 x 34 x 3.5 inches
Б─°Morning Mien,Б─² stoneware, 51 x 27 x 2 inches
Detail of Б─°Morning MienБ─²
Б─°Iron Centipede,Б─² soda-fired stoneware, 5 x 13 x 13 inches collapsed or up to 6 x 14 x 14 inches as a basket
Б─°Adobe Bulge,Б─² stoneware, 16.5 x 15 x 3 inches
Б─≤CentipedesБ─≥ series, ranging from 2 to 12 inches

Б─°Surrounded,Б─² approximately 2 x 28 inches diameter; when unfurled, a continuous tube of ceramic chainmail

title: Wildfires and War Rage in Shawn Huckins' Theatrical Paintings
description: In Slow Burn, Shawn Huckins puts the cognitive dissonance that defines our current era in stark relief. The New Hampshire-based painter has long c
body:
In Slow Burn, Shawn Huckins puts the cognitive dissonance that defines our current era in stark relief. The New Hampshire-based painter has long challenged American mythology and collective aggrandizing by reinterpreting canonical artworks and visual languages. His series have commented on the U.S.Б─≥s proclivity for erasing history and the ways our garments convey social status and class. In this new body of work, he directs us to the contradictory experience of witnessing destruction as both a spectacle and a distant occurrence. 
Slow Burn presents a suite of landscapes, each veiled by curtains. Floral drapery flanks a catastrophic explosion, a sliver of sunlight peeks through a decorative toile de jouy pattern, and delicate lace veils a wildfire in the distance. Opened just enough to allow a partial glimpse, the curtains suggest a theatrical scene in which the raging disasters of war and the climate crisis are ongoing.
Б─°War Cloud and Floral Blue CurtainБ─² (2026), oil and acrylic on canvas, 37 x 30 inches
In contrast to the 19th-century landscapes synonymous with the Hudson River School, Huckins stages scenes in which romanticism and idealized vistas are long gone. While the viewer peers outward from the safety of a home, the fires and bombs that characterize the outside world inch closer, and the complacency, privilege, and pure luck that have provided protection thus far are ever more precarious.
Slow Burn runs from July 11 to August 22 at K Contemporary in Denver. Until then, find more from the artist on Instagram.
Б─°Wildfire Sunset Behind Sheer CurtainБ─² (2026), acrylic on canvas, 60 x 48 inches
Б─°First Sun and Green CurtainБ─² (2026), oil on canvas, 64 x 96 inches
Б─°Wildfire Sunrise Behind Floral Lace CurtainБ─² (2026), acrylic on canvas, 36 x 30 inches
Б─°War Cloud and Glowing Blue CurtainБ─² (2026),  oil and acrylic on canvas, 60 x 48 inches
Б─°First Sun and Toile de Jouy Curtain (2)Б─² (2026), oil on canvas, 32 x 26 inches
Б─°Sunset and Glowing Green Curtain Study (after Church)Б─² (2026), oil and acrylic on canvas, 32 x 26 inches

title: Jeanne Vicerial's Ethereal Sculptures Dot Historic Spaces in Aix-en-Provence in 'Incarnation'
description: Softness and resilience. Presence and absence. Vitality and stillness. These are just a few of the dualities that permeate the atmospheric work of
body:
Softness and resilience. Presence and absence. Vitality and stillness. These are just a few of the dualities that permeate the atmospheric work of Jeanne Vicerial, whose textile-focused practice taps into history and femininity with precision and reverence.
A city-wide exhibition of VicerialБ─≥s pieces titled Incarnation: Carte blanche Jeanne Vicerial opens across several historic spaces in Aix-en-Provence this month: Musц╘e du Pavillon de VendцЄme, Musц╘e des Tapisseries, Chapelle de laб═Visitation, and Musц╘e Granet. Situated amid centuries-old architecture and existing museum collections, the artistБ─≥s works nod to time, tradition, and remembrance. The show surveys sculptures and installations created throughout the last several years, including sprawling installations that feature yards of pooling rope and smaller, intimate, provocative wall pieces.
Б─°Buste nб╟1 Minerva, Б─≤celle qui mesure'Б─² (2024), ropes, threads, bronze,  20 1/2 x 14 1/4 x 8 3/4 inches. Photo б╘ Laurent Edeline
Known for her dramatic Armors series, Vicerial nods to medieval European heritage, such as knightsБ─≥ armor and burial and memorial customs. Б─°Gisante de cе⌠ur,Б─² for example, which translates to Б─°recumbent figure of the heart,Б─² references the tradition of nobility or priestsБ─≥ sculptural likenesses placed atop sarcophagi. Except in VicerialБ─≥s world, these figures are all enigmatically, anonymously, and powerfully female. The silhouettes of bodies are conveyed through textile, which in turn is associated with clothing and its potential to both reveal and conceal as well as protect.
Б─°Beyond practical functions, textiles carry within them a memory: the memory of gestures, of uses, and of the traces left behind by the bodies that have worn or transformed them,Б─² says a statement. Б─°Thus, the material becomes a silent witness to past presencesБ─■the medium for a history that is simultaneously individual, intimate, and collective.Б─²
Incarnation opens on June 13 and continues through October 4.
Б─°Gisante (Amnios)Б─² (2022), ropes, threads, and varnished roses, 77 4/7 x 22 3/7 x 13 4/5 inches. Photo б╘ Adrien Millot
Б─°Armor nб╟5Б─² (2016-2021) textiles, yarns, cords, knitting, 70 7/8 x 21 5/8 x 17 5/7 inches. Photo б╘ Laurent Edeline
Б─°Sex voto ornц╘ nб╟19Б─² (2025), ropes, copper and brass gilded with fine gold, 9 3/4 x 5 x 3/4 inches. Photo б╘ Laurent Edeline
Б─°Gisante nб╟4 Б─≤ц┴lц╘a,Б─≥ Ce qui nБ─≥existe pas existeБ─² (2024), ropes, threads, 13 1/2 x 95 1/4 x 31 3/4 inches. Photo б╘ Laurent Edeline
Б─°Vц╘nus ouverte #2Б─² (2020), ropes, threads, tricotissage (labeled technique), 70 3/4 ц≈ 31 1/2 inches. Photo б╘ Laurent Edeline
Detail of Б─°Gisante de coeur.Б─² Photo б╘ Laurent Edeline
